item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is Ledenoff Technologies using this anomaly to sabotage people? Or do they think that Quaesturas is genuinely helping? Or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This gave me an eyegasm. That web design course paid off.

The story here is great. And it reminds me of why we don't use Agile at work anymore.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The formatting serves two puposes:This is much more computer-esque which has always bothered me that the Foundation is hypercompetent at almost everything, but apparently web design.

The main style is reminiscent of declassified CIA documents which where made on typewriters and carbon-copied. The style was popular in the X-Files and related media, which inspired much of the early SCP. The black-boxing is also from this style. I think it evokes the proper tone and setting for some pieces. It would be nonsensical for the foundation to continue with this style post-2010. Your style is also easier to read. It's clearly a good change here, but I'm indifferent on its use elsewhere. I can see both sides of this.

item.title: Re: Admin Post
item.content:encoded: Capiche. My apologies.

item.title: Admin Post
item.content:encoded: Peppersghost, I feel this comment crosses the line from constructive criticism, to purposeful dickishness. If you have something constructive to say about the article, that is not set up as passive aggressive sniping, you are free to do so, but this level of rudeness is not needed here, capiche?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not hard to read, just confusing in terms of purpose. And I'm also talking about the numerous headings that don't normally show up in articles; that's really more of what's slowing it down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is not what this format is for, the document is not affected by the anomaly. It's just a more visually appealing presentation.

I honestly don't get all the "this is hard to read" posts, but I designed it so maybe I don't get it.

I also don't find that a particularly valuable critique either, but everyone is entitled to express their opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The lines detailing who wrote the last revision, when they wrote it, and how to contact them are actually pretty seasonable, no probs there. However, I take issue with this format for the following reasons:

  • Breaking up the SCP document into individual cards doesn't help convey the information any better, and thus comes across as a contrivance.
  • Previously in the thread, you mentioned that this is meant to be read on the Foundation website, not in print. If this is the case, having the logo at the top is redundant.
  • Having the headers in large, red, bold text is overdoing it by quite a wide margin, and the underline makes it look like a hyperlink. Remember, when everything is emphasized, nothing is; you don't want researchers to become accustomed to skimming over large blocks of red text.
  • The item number and object class are floating on opposite sides of the page, which neither looks good nor helps readability.
  • The CSS of the collapsibles has been changed to the extent that they're unrecognizable, and I nearly overlooked them as a result.
  • The blue footnote at the end is inexplicable to me. If you mean to imply that the footnote itself is redacted, then putting it right next to the redaction box is pretty redundant.

I apologize for my unwarranted snark. I was absolutely projecting some repressed frustration with parties unrelated to the SCP wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really get it, and it seems I'm not the only one. I was put off by the (admittedly aesthetically pleasing) format at first, but by the end, I can only assume it's meant to be one of those "documentation affected by anomaly" type things. But it doesn't seem to mean anything, it just literally slows the article down and makes it a bit of a slog to read.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Due to some very vocal complaints about my apparent lack of web-style knowledge, I was forced to take a course in simple web design.

As such, I've updated the theme with a more modern, less-floating-in-the-internet update.

Hopefully you're happy now. You made me take a course in web design you fuckers.


<3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I missed the interconnection stuff because I'm nearly not as immersed into the SCP universe as I once was, but the article as it is is quite fun simply for its Dilbertesque feel. As someone who has experienced mild form of this in real life, I can't not upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This explains SO much, heheh.

+1, an enjoyable read. And while I'd hate it if the Foundation adopted that formatting for ease-of-readability reasons, it does work well for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sprinted to this article as soon as you mentioned it, and boy did it deliver. If I could, I'd give it a stand-up ovation. Each document was better than the previous iteration. Something something pair programming.

…seriously though, this was a blast to read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I was just kind of bored reading it :( Companies becoming clogged up with busywork until they can't actually do anything is kind of interesting, but…I didn't really feel anything from it. I read the comment thread, and apparently this sets up future things, but since I don't know what those are, I can only go by this article which, outside of some okay formatting (that comes with some specific issues on its own, see below), comes off as pretty flat, narratively and emotionally.

Now to you, I pose a question. Where have you heard the name "Ledenoff" before?

I, uh, hadn't, so anything dealing with whoever that is had no impact. :/

Note on formatting: I didn't care for all the red type (especially underlined red type), as that is generally a signifier for an active link on this site, as far as I'm concerned. Headers should be changed to another color to reduce confusion (such as a dark grey). Also, cards with drop shadows inside other cards with drop shadows seems like way too many drop shadows. Drop shadows are also…quite dated for an online environment, and elsewhere. Crisp, flat color is the way to go in these modern times. I also thought there was too much dead space in the top card. The logo looks tiny and awkwardly placed, the spacing between lines and the logo is all over the place and frankly, the entire layout of the top card is fairly sloppy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oof ouch owie
my bones

Ah okay, well I look forward to it, definitely enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't hurt your brain with brain hurting juice
Try bone hurting juice instead

To be fair, the actual significance of the date isn't important yet. I haven't written that bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a nice rabbit hole you have there.
Shame I can't jump down it yet.

Soooo can I find out the date-time-mystery from reading this series? Is it something to do with 2017 and the 17th floor of the Ledenoff building?? On second thought I'll get back to this after my final exam tomorrow…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
magic makes software companies bureaucratize themselves to a halt

Considering the amount of feedback I got from readers and critiquers in the tech field about how much this resonated with them, I disagree.

Maybe it's just a lack of contextual framework, but this is basically an exaggeration of a real life thing that happens all the time.

Audience resonance is something to write for, in my eyes. Maybe it's not some ultra-complex thought provoking article, but that wasn't the point. The point was to add another brick in a growing narrative, and write something that people can relate to, and resonate with.

If something has to be unsettling, weird, or subversive for you to consider it quality, we may have creative differences I doubt we'll reconcile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly is introduced, escalates, and the potential involvement of a GoI/PoI shows that there's more to this than just a one-off piece of software.

The PoI also shows that it's aware of the Foundation, and will most likely produce more anom-tech.

See, this is pretty much paint-by-the-numbers SCP writing. All of these, on their own and together, have been hashed and rehashed endlessly already. Your basic anomaly — magic makes software companies bureaucratize themselves to a halt — is nothing to write home about, so you're not doing it any favors by plugging it into a generic story.

So I guess to answer your question of "Where exactly would this have gone?", I would say "literally anywhere unexpected". There are no curveballs in this piece, nothing that forced me to reevaluate what I'd already read, nothing truly unsettling or weird… It only goes somewhere in the sense that moving from your bedroom to the kitchen is going somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I find truly impressive about this one is that it manages to be surprisingly approachable despite being both technical and part of a larger narrative.

Good stuff, dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this aesthetic, and the insideous nature of the anomaly. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To start, thank you for the in-depth feedback.

Unfortunately, this is one of those "Set up the later information" articles.

Spoilers ahead
Return to safety

Karl Ledenoff was Ruslav's teacher, before his "rebirth" in to his current artificial alchemic life-form…form.

Karl is also a very very prominent figure in the 4th Alchemic War series….its just not been shown yet.

I'm going to have the next "piece of the puzzle" so to speak out hopefully by the end of the month, but there's the valid criticism that this should stand on its own.

With that together (which will be back-inserted in to the redaction notice), a lot more of this will make sense.

I'm trying to build something interconnected, and it admittedly weakens each piece, not seeing them all at once.

Hope this illuminates some of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, too, enjoyed the format. Far more interesting than the current 'list'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice and sleek formatting is a definite plus. It's an alternate take on the standard format, but not enough so as to be a 'screw', which is something I'd like to see more of in the future (pretty presentation is always a plus). There's room for subtle storytelling in how it's formatted, as is the case here, so I didn't find that extra bit of content distasteful or much of a space-waste.

The article didn't really do much for me. As far as the corporate shenanigans go, it wasn't something I found particularly interesting in the least, and it doesn't seem to develop much past the initial premise for how long it turns out being. I noticed Diaghilev's name, so I got the connection, but I don't think the actual nature of the alchemical tie-in was really apparent, even after a reread. Closest I can figure was this bit:

come in to work, consume resources, and produce no viable work.

Which I took as a mere allusion and nothing more.

But even if I'm reading into it wrong or overlooking something entirely, and it is intended to be a magic ploy, then it's still too dry for my tastes, when we've already got SCP-910, which tackles a similar concept in a more interesting manner (if indeed this is supposed to be an alchemical anomaly revolving around organizations).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the format. Wish I could upvote multiple times for everything else. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're not wrong.

Still not apologizing. My article is a strong, materials design compliant webapp who don't need no physical media.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And thus, truth.
Begone!

Dirty is a strong word. Acting very suspicious though.

And man, is that a GREAT question about the new year. Shame I can't answer it.

The series involving ruslav starts finding balance here if you'd like to read more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoil it, I must know
– hide block

Ahhhh I see, so it's the hint that the Director is dirty and in with Ledenoff (or is he Ledenoff???) and the possible start of a linking of articles in the future? Why was it redacted right before the new year though?

Man I need to pay more attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I don't quite understand this one.

Where exactly would this have gone? The anomaly is introduced, escalates, and the potential involvement of a GoI/PoI shows that there's more to this than just a one-off piece of software.

The PoI also shows that it's aware of the Foundation, and will most likely produce more anom-tech.

I'm curious where you feel this would have "went", without becoming extraordinarily longer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers, dear doctor.
Hide

Not quite.

The last update timer is the important part.

Director Diaghilev redacted this document, to remove the reference to K. Ledenoff's activity in….something.

He shouldn't be doing that. This isn't his department after all. Why would the Director redact a technical anomaly's documentation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The timestamps include at least one important narrative point.

Look closely, and don't dismiss them as cruft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah I loved this on the forums when I saw it! So if I understand the formatting…

+spoiler
– hide block

The formatting is like this in part because of the infection of the SCP into the foundation team? Like the endless "prepared by" notes and times for not much reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See for me, I just thought it was pretty boring and didn't really go anywhere. At least Dilbert strips have punchlines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"If it is possible to do a task wrong, people will do it that way."
- The original Murphy's Law

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about the format just doesn't work with me. It doesn't seem to be necessary, and the timestamps and names at the bottom of each section definitely don't feel necessary. It seems like a way to visually take up space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…this is supposed to be a webpage view, not a paper copy.

If someone printed this, they're nuts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding the sentiment here.

As I mentioned in the critique thread, it's a very fancy looking bit of coding, but I'm not a fan at all. It's extremely noisy and distracting compared to the simple, clean, reader-friendly format already in place, and clashes with the old-school aesthetic of the site. (Not to mention that the in-universe budget department would launch a coup over the extra paper and ink eaten up printing all that filigree.)

Will make the suggestion that with some tweaking this could be salvaged as a great example of an SCP affected by its own anomaly, with increasingly distracting and complicated formatting and annotations introduced over the course of the article, making it harder and harder to actually read for meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same. I have to admit I didn't really get this. The only additional information I can see in the alternative formatting is a bunch of timestamps and user changes, which I found too dry to attempt to relate back to the SCP.

I'm probably missing something, but I also don't really get the SCP itself, beyond 'company sells anomalous software program that messes up corporate operations, and maybe the Foundation is affected too', which didn't make me feel much of anything. It reminds me of the other SCP about the anomalous organisational structure that damages productivity, but with the format distracting me from the content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also would like this to be standard, it looks so damn neat and pleasant to look at. The nice feeling I get when I look at it is what prompted me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fancy formatting threw me off and I skimmed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not particularly against any of the formatting in principle, but I think there's a few too many drop shadows in practice. Each individual section has one, plus the addenda collapsibles, and it feels very noisy to me as a result. Subjectivity and all that, but it made it hard for me to focus on where to read next whenever I was scrolling down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
mmm tasty box shadows +1

But seriously, good job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This formatting is really impressive. I wouldn't mind seeing it become the standard.

I like the anomaly here and I'm excited to see where this series is going - where it really clicked for me was seeing the term "stand-ups" for a meeting (new to me) and

They started getting longer. Like, a lot longer. We went from giving updates, to giving code reviews, to giving in depth discussions about methodology and style. It seemed so normal, like…for real. I didn't even notice.

I tend to think of what I do as behavioral engineering - designing and running therapy programs, with specific types of behavior and response as the inputs and outputs - and this exact thing with stand-up meetings affects every workplace I've ever been in. Your skip may have made the jump to infecting analogue systems :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking awesome to look at. A fantastic read as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is slick.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I finally decided to take the plunge.

This is part of the Alchemy Series, though at first glance it may not appear so. Pay close attention to the fancy formatting.

Thanks go to everyone who took a look at this, and gave me feedback on the forums.

The formatting serves two puposes:

  1. This is much more computer-esque which has always bothered me that the Foundation is hypercompetent at almost everything, but apparently web design.
  2. The formatting allows for several key pieces of information to be conveyed.

Now to you, I pose a question. Where have you heard the name "Ledenoff" before?

EDIT: This theme is publically available at ccastellano.github.io/magnus-theme.css To use it on your pages:

[[module]]
@import url(http://ccastellano.github.io/magnus-theme.css)
[[/module]]

From there, you can use all the div id's that correspond with these components.
